classification.title: CONTENTS XV PARKS
classification.summary: This document is a table of contents for the 1930 Kentucky General Assembly session laws, specifically listing chapters related to Parks and Public Health. The park-related chapters include the creation of the Kentucky National Park Commission, regulation of land jurisdiction for national parks, funding for Cumberland Falls State Park, eminent domain powers for park acquisition, park purchases by second-class cities, and appropriations for memorial parks and park commissions. The public health chapters cover the powers of the Board of Tubercular Hospitals and the revocation of optometrist licenses.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a table of contents for the 1930 Kentucky General Assembly session laws. It lists various acts related to parks and public health. None of the listed acts or their descriptions contain any explicit or implicit racial indicators. Therefore, this is not a Jim Crow law.

Stats., beginning with section 68b-1 and continuing to and through section 68b-37).

2 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
wealth of Kentucky of one thousand nine hundred
and eighteen ( 1918 ) approved March 28 , 1918 , and
entitled , " AN ACT relating to dogs , the promotion
of live stock industries , and the protection of live
stock and poultry from damage by dogs ; providing
for the licensing of dogs ; regulating the keeping of
dogs , and authorizing their destruction in certain
cases ; providing for the protection of licensed dogs
and for dogs temporarily imported for trial , show ,
and breeding purposes ; prescribing certain privi-
leges for hunting dogs ; providing for listing of dogs
by county assessors and penalty for his failure to
so list ; providing for the assessment of damages
done by dogs , and payment thereof by the proper
county to the owners of live stock and poultry , and
of damages to licensed dogs ; imposing powers and
duties on certain State and county officers and pro-
viding penalties and fees " , as amended by Chapter
one hundred and fifty - seven ( 157 ) of the Acts of the
General Assembly of 1920 , be amended and re -en-
acted so that the same when amended and re -enacted
shall read as follows :
1. That this act shall be known and may be cited
as an act to promote and protect live stock industries
in Kentucky .
2. For the purpose of this act the following
terms shall have the following meanings respectively
designated for each :
The term "live stock " shall include horses , stal-
lions , colts , geldings , mares , sheep , rams , lambs ,
bulls , bullocks , steer , heifers , cows , calves , mules ,
jacks , jennets , burros , goats , kids and swine .
The term " poultry " shall include all domestic
fowl .
The word " person " shall include State and local
officers or employes , individuals , corporations , co-

partnerships , and associations . Singular words

The word " owner " when applied to the proprie-
torship of a dog, shall include every person having
a right of property in such dog , and every person
who keeps or harbors such dog or has it in his care ,
and every person who permits such dog to remain
on or about any premises occupied by him .
3. The term " kennel " shall mean any establish-
ment wherein or whereon dogs are kept for the pur-
pose of breeding , sale or sporting purposes .
The term "police officer " shall mean any sheriff
or his deputies or any one appointed by the sheriff

WHEREAS , it has been shown that of the total area of the State
there are 10,500,000 acres classified as not suitable for anything
except the growing of timber , and whereas all of this land
should be growing just as much timber as it can produce , so
that future generations of Kentuckians will have lumber to
cut instead of bare hillsides , and whereas floods cause millions
of dollars worth of damage every year in Kentucky resulting
in much sickness and misery , and whereas forest fires are the
main factors in suppressing the young timber now trying to
grow on this said 10,500,000 acres , burning the ground cover ,
causing the slopes to erode and wash , filling up the stream
beds , and whereas there are large and valuable areas of second
growth timberlands in the State which are unprotected from
forest fires , due to the small annual appropriation allotted the

destroy thousands of dollars worth of timber yearly, it is felt

classification.title: CHAPTER 223
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for forest fire patrols on private forest lands in Kentucky. It mandates that landowners either conduct their own patrols or allow the State Forest Service to do so and charge the cost to the owner, with a limit of one cent per acre per year. The statute also details the process for collecting these charges and provides an exemption for landowners who are members of and contributing to existing fire protective associations. Additionally, it states that actual residence on a tract of 100 acres or less is considered adequate protection, but does not relieve the owner of responsibility for fire control or entitlement to compensation for suppression efforts.

deposit or any part thereof upon the personal check
of the fiduciary without being liable to the principal ,
unless the bank receives the deposit or pays the
check with actual knowledge that the fiduciary is
committing a breach of his obligation as fiduciary
in making such deposit or in drawing such check , or
with knowledge of such facts that its action in re-
ceiving the deposit or paying the check amounts to
bad faith .
$ 9. Nothing herein contained shall in any way
relieve a fiduciary who breaches his trust and causes
any loss thereby of his liability under his bond , or
of any civil or criminal liability provided for by law.
§ 10. The provisions of this Act shall not be re-
troactive .

Section 1155. Every male person who shall car-
nally know, with her consent , any female child , not
his wife , under the age of eighteen years , and every
female person who shall carnally know any male
child under the age of eighteen years , not her hus-
band , shall be punished as follows :
( 1 ) When such child is under the age of twelve
years , by imprisonment in the State Penitentiary
for not less than twenty years nor more than fifty
years , or by death , in the discretion of the jury .
(2) When such child is of the age of twelve years
and under the age of sixteen years , by imprisonment
in the State Penitentiary for not less than five years
nor more than twenty years .
(3) When such child is of the age of sixteen
years and under the age of eighteen years , by im-
prisonment in the State Penitentiary for not less
than two years nor more than ten years.
Provided , however , that in all prosecutions
for carnally knowing a female between the ages of
sixteen and eighteen years , it shall be permissible
to introduce evidence of any previous acts of sexual
immorality committed by said female , and her gen-
eral reputation for sexual immorality may be
proven , and where it is shown by the evidence
beyond a reasonable doubt that prosecutrix is sex-
ually immoral or of such reputation , then the de-
fendant shall only be guilty of a misdemeanor and ,
upon conviction , shall be fined not more than $500 .
(4) Any female under the age of eighteen years
and any male under the age of seventeen years ,
charged with a violation of any of the provisions of

§ 2. Administration may be dispensed with by
agreement in certain cases . ( one ) It shall not be
necessary to have administration upon the estate of

estate , or the written agreement provided in clause

claims and demands ; ( two ) The written agreement
provided in clause ( b ) of sub - section ( one ) must be
acknowledged by the parties and filed in the county
court together with the motion of the parties for
an order dispensing with administration . If the
court is satisfied by affidavit or otherwise that the
conditions prescribed in sub- section ( one ) exist , it
shall enter an order dispensing with administration .

AN ACT to amend Sections 20 and 25 of an act entitled , "An

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 20 of an act entitled , " An Act
concerning guardians , " approved December 3 , 1892 ,
being Section 2034 of the Kentucky Statutes , be
amended by substituting in subsection 2 thereof the
words , " support , maintenance or education , " for
the words , " board and tuition , " so that said section
as amended shall read as follows :
"No disbursements shall be allowed the guardian
for the support , maintenance or education of the
ward beyond the income of the estate , except in the
following cases , unless authorized by the deed or
will under which the estate is derived :
"(1) When the ward is of such tender years or
infirm health that he cannot be bound out as an ap-
prentice , or no suitable person will take him as such .
"(2) When it is best for the ward that the prin-
cipal of his personal estate shall be applied for his
support , maintenance or education , and the court ,
upon settlement of the accounts , shall deem such
application to have been judicious and properly
made . But neither the ward not his real estate shall
be liable for any such disbursement . "
2. That Section 25 of said " act concerning
guardians ," being Section 2039 of the Kentucky
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1930, p. 138

CHAPTER 33 .
AN ACT Concerning Estates Not Exceeding One Hundred
Dollars of Persons under Disability to Permit Payment to
Those Having Custody of Such Persons .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That in the settlement of any estate in the County
or Circuit court or in any action wherein real prop-
erty has been sold , if any person under disability
be entitled to share in the proceeds and such share
does not exceed one hundred dollars ($ 100.00 ) ex-
clusive of interest and such person has no guardian ,
or curator or committee , the court may order the
same to be paid to the person having custody of such
person under disability . Before entering the order
the court shall be satisfied by affidavit or oral testi-
mony that the custody of the person under disabil-
ity is had by the person to whom it is proposed to
pay the money and the latter , upon withdrawal of
the money , shall be under obligation as trustee to
expend the same for the support , maintenance or
education of the person under disability . If the
order is made , no bond shall be required for the
share of the person under disability and the pur-
chaser of the real estate may pay such share into
court , and no lien shall remain on the real estate
therefor and the money may be withdrawn by the
person mentioned in the order without his giving
bond .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

CHAPTER 35 97
8. No person , firm or corporation shall secure
or attempt to secure the adoption of any school
textbook or books in any school district in this Com-
monwealth by rewarding or promising to reward ,
directly or indirectly , any person in any public
school in the Commonwealth . No person , firm or
corporation shall offer or give any emolument to any
person in any school district for any vote or promise
to vote, or the use of his influence , for any school
textbook or books to be used in this Commonwealth .
§ 9. No superintendent , teacher , or other official ,
or employee of any institution supported wholly or
in part by public funds shall act , either directly or
indirectly , as agent for any person , firm or corpora-
tion whose school textbooks are filed with the Super-
intendent of Public Instruction , as provided by law .
Such sample textbooks as may be submitted to mem-
bers of the State Textbook Commission for exami-
nation shall continue to be the property of the bid-
der if claimed within 30 days after adoption or shall
be sold by the State Board of Education and the
money covered into the State school fund .
§ 10. Any person , firm or corporation violating
any of the provisions of this act shall, on conviction
thereof, be punished by a fine not to exceed five
hundred dollars ( $ 500.00 ) , or by imprisonment not
to exceed three months , or by both such fine and
imprisonment , at the discretion of the court .
§ 11. Whereas the schools in this Common-
wealth are now operating without a legal textbook
adoption , and whereas this act provides for ap
adoption to be made before July 1 , 1930 , an emer-
gency is hereby declared to exist and this act shall
become effective upon its passage and approval .
Disapproved March 6, 1930. Passed House of Representatives
March 6, 1930 , the Governor's veto to the contrary notwithstand-
ing . Passed Senate March 7, 1930 , the Governor's veto to the
contrary notwithstanding .

CHAPTER 36.
AN ACT relating to education in this Commonwealth and appro-
priating money from the General Fund of the Treasury of the
Commonwealth to be used for education purposes in connec-
tion with the public school system of this Commonwealth and
conferring authority and power upon the State Board of Edu-
cation to supervise and administer the expenditure of money
so appropriated , defining the purposes for which the money
so appropriated shall be used , and providing conditions upon
which it shall be received ; and generally to provide for a more
efficient system of public schools in this Commonwealth .
Be it enacted by General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
1. There is hereby appropriated annually out
of the General Fund of the State Treasury the sum
of one million two hundred and fifty thousand dol-
lars ( $1,250,000.00 ) for the purposes herein stated
and defined . The same shall be expended and ad-
ministered under the direction and supervision of
the State Board of Education , as provided in this
Act.
Section la . The same shall be expended under
direction and supervision of the State Board of
Education as determined by the majority of its
members acting in corporate capacity and as pro-
vided in this Act .
§ 2. The money herein appropriated shall be
known and designated as a fund for the equalization
of educational opportunities in this Commonwealth .
Equalization of educational opportunities , as used
in this Act, means the raising of the level of expen-
ditures per pupil for educational purposes in school
districts where the level of educational opportuni-
ties is below the level or standard fixed and pre-
scribed by law and by the State Board of Education
as hereinafter provided . This equalization relates
to funds expended other than the per capita distri-

CHAPTER 42 .
AN ACT regulating the time for a six-year -old child to enter
school .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That any child who becomes six years of age dur-
ing any school year shall have the privilege of en-
tering school at the first of the session , provided
such child will be six years of age within three
months of the opening date of the session of school ;
provided , however , that no board of education shall
by law be compelled to permit any six-year -old child
to enter school who has reached its sixth birthday
more than three months after the opening of the
school session .
Approved March 26 , 1930 .

plement , be , and the same is , hereby repealed .

§ 1. That all persons now holding positions of
superintendent or supervisor in the public schools
of this Commonwealth having certificates entitling
them to hold such position or employment , which
expire on or before June 30 , 1930 , who have served
not less than one full term of four years in such po-

perience of such superintendents or supervisors

for reissue of such certificates , and for holding such
positions or offices .

sors are appointed and shall have qualified . On the
1st day of January in the year 1934 , and every
fourth year thereafter , two members of said Board
shall be appointed in the manner herein provided ,
who shall hold office for a term of four years and
until their successors are appointed and shall have
qualified . Members of said Board shall be eligible
for reappointment and to succeed themselves .

classification.title: CHAPTER 48 125
classification.summary: This statute outlines the qualifications and appointment process for registration officers in Kentucky for the 1930 general initial precinct registration. It specifies that officers must not have been convicted of a felony, must be sober, temperate, discreet, and of good demeanor, not be a candidate in the upcoming election, and be capable of reading the Kentucky Constitution in English and writing legibly. The statute also details the partisan appointment of deputy registrars and inspectors, requiring them to be of opposite political affiliations within each precinct. Compensation for these officers is set at five dollars per day. The Board has the power to remove disqualified officers.
classification.reasoning: The statute itself does not explicitly mention race. However, the historical context of Jim Crow laws in Kentucky (1865-1966) suggests that such detailed regulations around voter registration, particularly the emphasis on literacy and 'good demeanor,' were often used as tools to disenfranchise Black voters. The requirement to read the Constitution in English and write legibly, while seemingly neutral, was a common barrier erected by Jim Crow laws. The partisan appointment system, while ostensibly about political balance, could also be manipulated to exclude certain groups. Given the period and the nature of voter registration laws, this statute is likely a component of the broader Jim Crow system, even without explicit racial language. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow with a confidence of 0.7, and 'implicit' for racial indicator. Human review is recommended due to the lack of explicit racial language.

registration is being conducted solely for the pur-

names and addresses of persons registered , together
with such other information as may be desired .
$ 17. From and after the time the office of the

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 1596a -12 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes , 1922 edition , relating to contested elections .
classification.summary: This act amends Section 1596a-12 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1922 edition, concerning contested elections. It modifies the timeframes for initiating a recount and the service of summons in election contests. Specifically, it changes the period for requesting an immediate recount from "within thirty days after the final action of the board of canvassers" to "within thirty days after day of election." It also adjusts other timeframes related to the contest process, such as changing "thirty" to "sixty," "issues are made up" to "service of summons," "twenty" to "fifty," and "thereafter" to "after filing of answer."

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :

vided , contest an election , or demand a recount of

county , city or district , for their approval or rejec-
tion. Any elector having the right to vote on the
question submitted , and having voted thereon , may
within thirty days after the election , file a petition
against the county , city or district in which the
election was held setting forth the grounds of the
contest , or reasons for requesting a recount , with
the Circuit Clerk of the county in which the election

ing of illegal votes , the exclusion of legal votes , the
unfair or illegal conduct of the election , tampering
with the returns , or the alteration of the certificates
of the results , or bribery , fraud , intimidation or cor-
rupt practices , or upon any ground tending to frus-
trate , obstruct or interfere with the free expression
of the will of the voters . The Circuit Clerk at such
county shall forthwith order the County Board of
Election Commissioners and the county clerk in the
county involved in such contest to preserve and hold

which the recount is demanded sent to the court
house of said county , in such manner as said judge
may designate , and said judge shall designate two

(b) The court shall pass on all disputes respect-
ing ballots and shall ascertain the result of said
election after counting all legal ballots . If it shall
appear that the result as certified is not correct , the
correct result shall be ascertained and declared by
the court and certified to the canvassing board, and
the judgment shall supersede the official returns and
be given full effect . If it appear to the satisfaction
of the court that the election , or the returns thereof ,
were materially affected by fraud , duress , bribery ,
intimidation or corrupt practices the entire election
and the returns thereof shall be declared void and
ineffective for any purpose . Costs shall follow the
judgment as in equity cases .
§ 4. The circuit court of the county in which the
contested election was held shall have exclusive jur-
isdiction to hear and determine all matters in con-
test cases filed under the provisions of this act , and
an appeal to the Court of Appeals may be prose-
cuted by the unsuccessful party in the same manner
as now provided by law for appeals in other con-
tested election cases . But no appeal to the Court of
Appeals shall lie from the recount of the ballots ,
provided that any questioned ballots may be made
a part of the record and the ruling thereon by the
Circuit Court reviewed , revised or reversed by the
Court of Appeals .
§ 5. All laws and parts of laws in conflict here-
with are hereby repealed . Approved March 27 , 1930 .

180 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That every insurance company , doing busi-
ness in the State of Kentucky , and now required
by law or hereinafter required by law to make
deposits , of stocks , bonds , notes , real estate , mort-
gages or other evidence of indebtedness or owner-
ship of property for the protection and benefit of its
policy holders , shall make such deposits with the
Insurance Commissioner of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky , who shall hold them in his official capa-
city for the security and benefit of the policyholders
of the company making such deposits and also as
trustee for the insurance company .
The Insurance Commissioner shall value said
securities , properties or evidence of indebtedness at
their fair value when deposited and if, at any time
in the opinion of the Insurance Commissioner , the
fair value of said securities or properties shall have
declined below the value first fixed , he shall , in writ-
ing by registered mail , notify the insurance company
at its home office of said fact and shall require it
within thirty days to deposit other securities or
properties whose fair value shall be determined by
the Insurance Commissioner and shall in no event
be less than the fair value of the securities when
first deposited .
If any such insurance company declines or fails
to make the deposit as herein provided , its right to
do business in the Commonwealth of Kentucky shall
cease upon written notice to it by the Insurance
Commissioner and the said Commissioner shall
notify all agents of said company authorized to act
for it of that fact and shall also immediately notify
the Insurance Commissioner of all States where the
company is authorized to do business of that fact .
·

CHAPTER 67 .
AN ACT to assist ex-service men and women of Kentucky , who
do not come under the provisions of the act known as the
Kentucky Disabled Ex -Service Men's Board , in obtaining from
the Federal Government the relief covered by Acts of Con-
gress ; creating for that purpose the Ex-Service Men's Com-
mission of Kentucky , and appropriating money therefor ; pro-
viding for the maintenance of an office , stenographic and
clerical help to carry on the work of the Commission .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That there is hereby created a Commission
to be known as the Ex - Service Men's Commission
of Kentucky , which shall continue in existence for
the period of two years after the passage and
approval of this act . The purpose of the Commis-
sion is to assist ex-service men and women in Ken-

Men's Board , and who are , or may hereafter be,
entitled to aid from the United States Government ,

quarters of the Commission shall be located in a
city of the first class in the State of Kentucky .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. The sum appropriated under the provisions
of this act shall be used for the sole purpose of pro-
viding for matriculation fees , board and room rent
and books and supplies for the use and benefit of the
children not under sixteen and not over twenty -one
years of age , and who have for twelve months had
their domicile in the State of Kentucky , of those who
were killed in action or died from other cause in the
World War , from April 6 , 1917 , to July 2 , 1921 ,
while serving in the army , navy or marine corps of
the United States ; which children are attending or
may attend a state educational or training institu-
tion of a secondary or college grade . Said children
shall be admitted to state institutions of secondary
or college grade free of tuition .
§ 2. The amounts that may be or may become
due to any such educational or training institution ,
not in excess of the amount specified in section three
hereof, shall be payable to such institutions from the
fund hereby created on vouchers aproved by the
State Board of Education . Said board shall deter-
mine the eligibility of the children who may make
application for the benefits provided for in this act ;
satisfy itself of the attendance of such children at
any such institution and of the accuracy of the
charge or charges submitted to said board by the
authorities of any such institution on account of the
attendance thereat of any such children ; provided no
member of said board or the secretary shall receive
any compensation for such services .
3. The sum of three thousand six hundred
dollars , or so much thereof as may be necessary , is
hereby appropriated from the general fund for each

30, 1932 , for carrying out the provisions of this act ;

CHAPTER 72 .
AN ACT to amend section 1376a so as to provide penalties
against the unauthorized wearing of or improper use of
badges or insignia of the American Legion , the American
Legion Auxiliary or the American War Mothers.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 1376a , Carroll's 1922 Edition
of the Kentucky Statutes , be , and the same is hereby
amended by inserting after the words " or other
similar secret societies " the following : " The Ameri-
can Legion , the American Legion Auxiliary or the
American War Mothers , " and that said section , as
so amended , be re -enacted so as to read as follows :
" That any person who shall willfully wear the
badge of the Grand Army of the Republic , Masonic ,
Odd Fellows , Knights of Pythias , Knights of Honor ,
Confederate Veterans , or other similar secret socie-
ties , American Legion , American Legion Auxiliary ,
or American War Mothers , or who shall wear the
same , to obtain aid or assistance thereby , within this
state , unless he shall be entitled to wear or use the
same , under the rules and regulations governing
said organization , shall be guilty of a misdemeanor ,
and, upon conviction , shall be punished by imprison-
ment for a term not exceeding thirty days in the
county jail , or a fine not to exceed twenty dollars , or
by both such fine and imprisonment . "
Approved February 26 , 1930 .

CHAPTER 73 .
AN ACT to authorize the Governor to receive as trustee from
the Secretary of War of the United States , and distribute as
trustee acording to his judgment , certain "Other Funds " re-
maining to the credit of such National Guard units of Ken-
tucky as have not been reconstituted .
WHEREAS , it has been duly made known to the proper authori-
ties of this State that the Secretary of War of the United
States has in his possession , as trustee , certain moneys known
as "Other Funds " which had been collected for their own use
and benefit by certain National Guard organizations that were
broken up as units for or as the result of service in the World
War , and have not been reconstituted ; and
WHEREAS , it further appears that the Secretary of War , as
trustee , desires to turn over to a substitute trustee , duly
authorized by this State , such portion of the said funds as
equitably belongs to the National Guard of this State ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That the Governor is hereby authorized to receive
such funds as trustee , and to distribute them for the
benefit of the National Guard of this State , in such
manner as his judgment shall dictate .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

It also addresses fees for interstate motor vehicles operating within a limited distance in the state.

classification.title: Amendment to Section 2739g-51 regarding vehicle speed limits
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 2739g-51 of the Kentucky statutes to establish different prima facie speed limits for vehicles on public highways based on the location (business district, residential area, sharp curve, steep grade, or unobstructed straightaway) and type of vehicle (trucks of one ton or less). It sets limits of 15 mph in closely built-up business portions, 20 mph in residential portions or on curves/grades, and 40 mph on unobstructed straightaways outside business sections.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an amendment to a statute concerning vehicle speed limits on public highways. It establishes different speed limits based on the type of road and its location (e.g., business district, residential area, curves, grades). There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to amending a specific section of the statutes enacted during a legislative session. classification.summary: This act outlines the procedure for serving legal process on non-resident defendants in Kentucky. It specifies that process should be issued to the sheriff of Franklin County, who will then serve the Secretary of State. The Secretary of State is responsible for notifying the non-resident defendant via registered mail, enclosing a copy of the summons and petition. The act also details the fees involved and allows for continuances to ensure the defendant has a fair opportunity to appear.

CHAPTER 81,
AN ACT to amend and re -enact an Act known as chapter 87 of
the Acts of 1922 , entitled , "An Act to amend sub -section C
of section 2, chapter 90, page 415 of the Acts of the General
Assembly of 1920 , regulating fees for passenger automobiles ,"
and further regulating fees for passenger automobiles .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That chapter 87 of the Acts of 1922 , entitled ,
"An Act to amend sub -section C of section 2 chap-
ter 90 , page 415 of the Acts of the General Assembly
of 1920 , regulating fees for passenger automobiles "
be and the same is hereby amended so that same
when amended and re -enacted shall read as follows :
Fees for Passenger Automobiles -For each mo-
torcycle registration fee shall be five dollars .
§ 2. Automobiles primarily designed for carry-
ing passengers and having provisions for not more
than seven passengers , including the operator , shall
be classed as passenger automobiles , and the annual
registration fee for same shall be nineteen cents for
each horsepower of the vehicle registered , to be cal-
culated on the rating fixed by the Society of Auto-
mobile Engineers , plus thirty cents on each the one
hundred pounds of said automobile .

CHAPTER 83 .
AN ACT to require all drivers of motor vehicles transporting
school children to stop each and every such motor vehicle
before crossing over any steam or electric interurban railroad
main tracks at a grade , except such grade crossing be pro-
tected by gates or flag operated by employees of the railroads ,
and direction precautions that drivers of motor vehicles trans-
porting school children shall take at grade crossings .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That all drivers of motor vehicles transporting
school children are hereby required to stop each and
every such motor vehicle before crossing over any
steam or electric interurban railroad main tracks at
a grade, stop to be made at not less than ten ( 10 )
feet nor more than thirty ( 30 ) feet from the nearest
railroad over which the highway crosses , except
where such crossing is guarded , crossing protected
by gates or a flag controlled or operated by em-
ployees of such railroad . After making the stop
herein required , the driver or operator of a motor
vehicle shall carefully look in each direction for
approaching cars or trains and shall not start his
vehicle until it is ascertained that no cars or trains
are approaching in either direction . Any person

CHAPTER 84.
AN ACT authorizing refund of motor vehicle license in event
such motor vehicles is destroyed by fire or accident .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That if any motor vehicle upon which the
license tax has been duly paid according to law shall
be destroyed by fire or accident so that such vehicle
shall be rendered entirely useless and unfit for ser-
vice , the owner thereof shall be entitled to have re-
funded to him that portion of the license fee paid by
him represented by the unexpired part of the calen-
dar year for which the license was granted .
§ 2. In order to obtain the refund provided for
in this act , the owner whose car has been destroyed
shall make application therefor to the Auditor of
Public Accounts , accompanying the same by his own
affidavit and the affidavits of two other reputable
persons , attesting to the fact that such vehicle has
been destroyed , and the manner of such destruction ,
and shall return the original license plates to the

CHAPTER 85.
AN ACT releasing owners of motor vehicles from responsibility
for injuries to passengers therein .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
First : No person transported by the owner or
operator of a motor vehicle , as his guest , without
payment for such transportation shall have a cause
of action for damages against such owner or opera-
tor for any injuries received , death , or any loss sus-
tained , in case of accident , unless such accident shall
have resulted from an intentional act on the part of
said owner or operator .
Approved March 21 , 1930 .

classification.summary: This act states that a person transported as a guest in a motor vehicle without payment for the transportation cannot sue the owner or operator for injuries, death, or loss sustained in an accident, unless the accident was intentional.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute, Chapter 85 of the 1930 Acts of the Kentucky General Assembly, deals with the liability of motor vehicle owners for injuries to passengers. It establishes a 'guest statute' that limits the ability of passengers to sue for damages unless the accident was intentional. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply universally to all passengers regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Section Two of an Act entitled "An Act to amend an Act
entitled 'An Act for the government of cities of the first class ,'
approved July first, one thousand eight hundred and ninety-
three ," and which was approved and became a law on March
21 , 1906 , as amended by Section 54 of an Act , the short title
of which is "City Government Bill- Cities of the first class ,"
which was approved and became a law March 22 , 1926 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Definitions . For the purpose of this Act
certain terms are defined , as provided in this Sec-
tion .
Whenever appropriate , the singular includes the
plural and the plural includes the singular . " Muni-
cipality " or " municipal " includes or relates to any
city of the first class in this Commonwealth .
" Mayor " means the chief executive of the City ,
whether the official designation of his office be
Mayor , City Manager , or otherwise . " Council '
means the chief legislative body of the municipality .
The term " streets " includes streets , avenues , boule-
vards , roads , lanes , alleys , viaducts and other ways .
" Subdivision " means the division of a lot , tract or
parcel of land into two or more lots , plats , sites , or
other divisions of land for the purpose , whether
immediate or future , of sale or of building develop-
ment . It includes re -subdivision and , when appro-
priate to the context , relates to the process of sub-
dividing or to the land or territory subdivided .
TITLE I-MUNICIPAL PLANNING AND ZONING
COMMISSIONS
§ 2. Grant of Power to Municipality .
Planning . Any city of the first class is hereby
authorized and empowered to make , adopt , amend ,
extend , add to or carry out a municipal plan , as pro-
vided in this Act , and create by ordinance a plan-

The commission is tasked with conducting surveys and studies of present conditions and future growth.

8. Procedure of Commission .
The Council may adopt a plan as a whole by a
single ordinance or may by successive ordinances
adopt successive parts of the plan , said parts cor-
responding with major geographical sections or divi-
sions of the municipality or with functional sub-

the Council shall hold at least one public hearing
thereon , notice of the time and place of which shall
be given by one publication in a newspaper of gen-
eral circulation in the municipality and in the official
gazette , if any , of the municipality . The adoption
of the plan or of any such part or amendment or
extension or addition shall be by ordinance of the
Council carried by the affirmative votes of not less
than a majority of members of the Council . The
ordinance shall refer expressly to the maps and
descriptive and other matters intended by the Coun-
cil to form the whole or part of the plan , and the
action taken shall be recorded on the map and plan
and descriptive matter by the identifying signature
of the President and Clerk of the Council . An at-
tested copy of the plan or part thereof shall be cer-
tified to the County Clerk for record .
§ 9. Legal Status of Official Plan.

of the illegality . Such petition shall be presented
to the court within thirty ( 30 ) days after the filing
of the decision in the office of the board .
Upon the presentation of such petition the court
may allow a writ of certiorari directed to the board
of adjustment and appeals to review such decision
of the board of adjustment and appeals and shall
prescribe therein the time within which a return
thereto must be made and served upon the relator's
attorney , which shall not be less than ten ( 10 ) days )
and may be extended by the court . The allowance
of the writ shall not stay proceedings upon the de-
cision appealed from , but the court may , on appli-
cation , on notice to the board and on due cause
shown , grant a restraining order .
The board of adjustment and appeals shall not
be required to return the original papers acted upon
by it, but it shall be sufficient to return certified or
sworn copies thereof or of such portions thereof as
may be called for by such writ . The return shall
concisely set forth such other facts as may be per-
tinent and material to show the grounds of the de-
cision appealed from and shall be verified .
If, upon the hearing, it shall appear to the court
that testimony is necessary for the proper disposi-
tion of the matter , it may take evidence or appoint
a referee to take such evidence as it may direct and
report the same to the court with his findings of
fact and conclusions of law , which shall constitute a
part of the proceedings upon which the determina-
tion of the court shall be made . The court may
reverse or affirm , wholly or partly , or may modify
the decision brought up for review.
Costs shall not be allowed against the board unless
it shall appear to the court that it acted with gross
negligence , or in bad faith , or with malice in making
the decision appealed from .

TITLE III- SUBDIVISION CONTROL
§ 14. Subdivision Jurisdiction .
The territorial jurisdiction of any municipal
planning commission over the subdivision of land
shall include all land located in the municipality and
all land lying within five miles of the corporate
limits of the municipality and not located in any
other municipality of the first class , except that , in
the case of any such nonmunicipal land lying within
five miles of more than one municipality having a
planning commission under this Act , the jurisdiction
of each such municipal planning commission shall
terminate at a boundary line equidistant from the
respective corporate limits of such municipalities .

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for cities to create and modify official maps showing streets and highways. It grants legislative bodies the power to change or add to these maps for public interest, including laying out new streets, widening, or closing existing ones. The map is considered final and conclusive regarding street locations and widths, established to promote public health, safety, and general welfare. It requires public hearings with at least ten days' notice before any changes and mandates referral to a planning board for a report. The statute also prohibits issuing building permits in the bed of mapped streets to preserve the integrity of official maps, with provisions for variances if the land cannot yield a fair return.

classification.title: Major Street Improvement Act
classification.summary: This act allows cities of the first class in Kentucky, which have adopted a major street plan, to widen, alter, extend, or open new major streets in accordance with that plan. It provides a method for property condemnation for these purposes and establishes special taxing districts to assess the costs of such improvements against benefited property owners. The collected taxes are made a prior lien on the property, immediately after state, county, and ordinary city taxes.

CHAPTER 89 .
AN ACT to amend , revise and re -enact Section 133 of an Act
relating to and amending the charter of cities of the first -class ,
the short title of which is , "City Government Bill- Cities of
the First Class ," approved March 22, 1926 , and being Chapter
115 of the Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky for 1926 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 133 of an Act of the General
Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky , ap-
proved March 22 , 1926 , and published as Chapter
115 of the Acts of the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky for 1926 , the short title of
which is , " City Government Bill-Cities of the First
Class , " be and the same is hereby amended , revised
and re -enacted , so that said Section 133 when so
amended , revised and re -enacted will read as fol-
lows :
(a) There is hereby created , effective from and
after the election and qualification of a Mayor in
the year 1929 , in cities of the first class , the office
of Accountant, and all the powers , obligations and
duties formerly vested in the office of Auditor in
cities of the first class and the incumbent thereof ,
together with any right of action , shall be vested in
the Accountant for said cities and who shall be ap-
pointed as provided herein .

CHAPTER 91 .
AN ACT relating to the government of cities of the second class
in the Commonwealth of Kentucky .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That any city of the second class may be-
come organized and be governed under the provi-
sions of this act by proceeding as hereinafter pro-
vided. Organizing under this act shall not change
the corporate entity of any such city , but the body
politic and corporate shall remain the same body
that it now is .
§ 2. All laws applicable to and governing cities
of the second class and not inconsistent with the
provisions of this act shall continue to apply to and
govern each city that may organize under this act ;
and all by-laws, ordinances and resolutions in force
in any such city and not inconsistent with the pro-
visions of this act shall continue to be in force until
altered or repealed in manner provided for in this
act .
§ 3. It shall be the duty of the county judge in
the county in which is located a city of the second
class proposing to take the sense of its voters , as
herein provided , upon the question of organizing
and being governed under this act , upon the appli-
cation by written petition signed by a number of
legal voters of said city equal to twenty per centum
(20% ) of the votes cast in said city at the last pre-
ceding election for mayor , to receive said petition ,

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 3096 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Street and Sidewalk Improvements
classification.summary: This statute, an amendment to Section 3096 of the Kentucky Statutes, outlines the process and funding for municipal street, alley, public way, and sidewalk improvements. It specifies that such improvements, including original construction, reconstruction, or resurfacing, shall be funded by the owners of abutting real estate, with costs apportioned based on front footage. A tax will be levied on these properties, becoming due upon completion and acceptance of the work. A penalty of ten percent will be added to taxes not paid within thirty days of becoming due. The statute also includes a procedural requirement for cities with bicameral councils, stating that ordinances for original street or sidewalk improvements cannot pass both boards on the same day, with a minimum two-week interval between passages.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the

between intersecting streets .
Any person , firm or corporation violating this act

nor more than five hundred ( $ 500.00 ) dollars and
any attempt to so erect a filling station in violation
of this act may be abated as a nuisance .
Nothing in this act shall be construed to prevent
the operation of any filling station erected prior to
this enactment .
All laws, or parts of laws , in conflict herewith are
hereby repealed .

CHAPTER 106 .
AN ACT to amend Section 3713 , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's
1922 Edition .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That Section 3713 , Kentucky Statutes , Car-
roll's 1922 Edition , be amended so that said section
as amended shall read as follows :
No such city shall become incorporated unless it
contains at least one hundred and twenty-five in-
habitants residing within the boundaries proposed
to be established for such city ; nor shall the boun-
dary of any city, when incorporated , exceed one-half
of a mile in each direction , the form of the city being
square : provided , however , that any unclassified
municipality or incorporated taxing district having
by its charter a grant of municipal powers and now
exercising the same , and having at least two hundred
and fifty inhabitants residing with (within ) the
boundary proposed to be established for such city,
may be incorporated as a city with its present ter-
ritorial boundaries .
§ 2. All acts or parts of acts in conflict herewith ,
to the extent of such conflict , are hereby repealed .
Approved March 25 , 1930 .

classification.title: An Act increasing and fixing the salaries of the First Assistant Commonwealth's Attorney and Second Assistant Commonwealth's Attorney in counties of this Commonwealth having a population of two hundred thousand, or over.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law (Acts of 1924, Chapter 24) to increase the annual salaries of the First Assistant Commonwealth's Attorney and the Second Assistant Commonwealth's Attorney in counties with a population of 200,000 or more. The new salary for each position is fixed at $4,000 per annum, payable monthly from the county levy. These assistant attorneys are to be appointed by the Commonwealth's Attorney for a four-year term but are subject to removal at any time by the Commonwealth's Attorney. They must be citizens of Kentucky and reside in the district where the Commonwealth's Attorney is elected.

CHAPTER 115 .
AN ACT to require the Governor to give written notice to the
Senate of the names of all appointments made by him where
the law now requires , or may hereafter require , said appoint-
ments to be made with the advice and consent of the Senate ,
within ten days before Senate adjourns , and providing that
on his failure to give such notice that a vacancy shall exist ,
which shall be filled by the Lieutenant Governor of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky . Also providing that when nomina-
tions to such office or offices are made and notices thereof
sent to the Senate which it fails or refuses to confirm that
a vacancy shall exist, and providing that the person whom
the Senate fails or refuses to confirm shall be ineligible for
appointment during a time when the Senate is not in session .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. It shall be the duty of the Governor to sub-
mit to the Senate a written list , ten days before ad-
journment of the Senate , of all of the persons ap-
pointed to office or offices where the Statutes now or
may hereafter require to be made with the advice
and consent of the Senate .

classification.title: AN ACT to require the Governor to give written notice to the Senate of the names of all appointments made by him where the law now requires , or may hereafter require , said appointments to be made with the advice and consent of the Senate , within ten days before Senate adjourns , and providing that on his failure to give such notice that a vacancy shall exist , which shall be filled by the Lieutenant Governor of the Commonwealth of Kentucky . Also providing that when nominations to such office or offices are made and notices thereof sent to the Senate which it fails or refuses to confirm that a vacancy shall exist , and providing that the person whom the Senate fails or refuses to confirm shall be ineligible for appointment during a time when the Senate is not in session .
classification.summary: This act mandates that the Governor must provide written notice to the Senate of all appointments requiring Senate consent at least ten days before the Senate adjourns. Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That section 3758 , Kentucky Statutes , Carroll's
Edition 1922 , be amended and re - enacted , and when
so amended and re -enacted shall read as follows :
1. The following officers shall have commis-
sions issued to them by the Governor , that is to say:
Secretary of State , Register of the Land Office ,
Auditor of Public Accounts , Treasurer , Commis-
sioner of Agriculture , Labor and Statistics , Super-
intendent of Public Instruction , Judges of the Court
of Appeals , Clerk of the Court of Appeals , Judges
of the Circuit Courts , Police Judges , Railroad Com-
missioners , Commonwealth's Attorney , Justices of
the Peace , Notaries Public , and all the officers of the
militia of rank and grade higher than and including
the rank and grade of captain. Should a vacancy
occur in any of said offices , by reason of the death ,
resignation or removal of the officer , or from any
other cause, or should a like vacancy occur in any
other office where there is no provision of law for
filling same , such vacancy shall be filled by the
appointment of the Governor , subject to the pro-
visions of the Constitution applicable thereto .
§ 2. Whenever a vacancy shall occur for any
reason whatsoever in the office of county judge in
any county of this State such vacancy shall be filled
by appointment made by the Fiscal Court of such
county ; such appointee to hold said office and dis-
charge its duties until his successor is elected and
qualified as is provided by law . A majority of the
members of such fiscal court shall constitute a
quorum for the purpose of filling such vacancy .
Such vacancy may be so filled by the fiscal court at
a regular meeting of that body , or at a special meet-
ing called for that purpose . Provided if the fiscal

That in all counties of this commonwealth having
therein cities of the second class , there is hereby
created the office of district detective to the Com-
monwealth's Attorney . Said district detective shall
be appointed by the Commonwealth's Attorney for
a term of four years , but may be removed at any

CHAPTER 120 .
AN ACT to amend and re -enact Section 911 of Kentucky Stat-
utes , 1930 Edition , being Chapter 157 of the Acts of the Gen-
eral Assembly of 1928 .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
That Section 911 of the Kentucky Statutes , 1930
Edition , being Chapter 157 of the Acts of the Gen-
eral Assembly of 1928 , be amended and re - enacted
so that when said section is so amended and re-
enacted shall read as follows :
" The official indexer appointed as aforesaid shall
receive for his services a sum fixed by the judges
appointing and confirming him not exceeding the
constitutional limitation , and said indexer may
appoint his own assistants subject to the approval
of the county judge but their compensation shall be
fixed by the official indexer with the approval of the
county judge and such compensation shall be paid
as the salary of the county judge is paid : Provided ,
however , that at no time shall the total expense
annually , under this act , exceed the sum of sixteen
thousand five hundred dollars ( $ 16,500.00 ) . This
shall apply only to counties having cities of the first
class . "
Neither approved nor disapproved .

CHAPTER 121 .
AN ACT relating to the compensation to be paid city attorneys
in cities of the fourth , fifth and sixth classes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That in addition to the compensation that
may be allowed city attorneys by the legislative
bodies of cities of the fourth , fifth and sixth classes ,
for his other legal services , he may, at the option of
the city council , be allowed as compensation for his
services in the police court thirty per centum ( 30 % )
of all fines and forfeitures recovered by him and
paid into the city treasury . Provided , however , that
if the fine be for a sum not exceeding twenty- five
dollars ( $ 25.00 ) exclusive of costs , a fee of five dol-
lars ( $5.00 ) shall be allowed him and taxed as costs
against the defendant . Said commission shall be
paid to the said city attorneys at the next council
meeting after the collection of such fines and for-
feitures .
§ 2. All laws and parts of laws in conflict here-
with are hereby repealed .
Approved March 17 , 1930 .

CHAPTER 127 .
AN ACT relating to the office of Assistant Secretary of State .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
The Assistant Secretary of State shall receive an
annual salary of twenty -four hundred ( $ 2,400.00 )
dollars , to be paid monthly as the salaries of other
State officers are paid , and he shall be subject to
removal from office at the pleasure of the Secretary
of State , with the approval of the Governor .
All acts and parts of acts in conflict herewith , to
the extent of such conflict , are hereby repealed .
Approved March 27 , 1930 .

classification.title: An Act to Amend Section 2 of Chapter 164 of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1928, Relating to the Mammoth Cave National Park Commission
classification.summary: This act amends a previous statute concerning the composition and appointment of the Mammoth Cave National Park Commission. It specifies that the commission will have nine members appointed by the Governor from a list of eighteen names recommended by the Mammoth Cave National Park Association. The Governor will also be an ex-officio member. Members must be citizens and residents of Kentucky with recognized business experience, serving two-year terms. The act outlines procedures for filling vacancies, taking oaths of office, and organizing the commission, including electing officers and adopting by-laws.

The purpose of the creation of this Commission
is to further the establishment of and to make pos-
sible a national park or national parks in Kentucky ,
in such places or areas as the Congress of the United
States has or may hereafter designate . To accom-
plish said purposes , the Commission is hereby
authorized and empowered to enter into contracts
and agreements with the United States relating to
the survey , location , and conveyance of property
which has been or which may be acquired by the
Commission for said purposes ; and to fully co-
operate with the United States and to do and cause
to be done all necessary acts and deeds to provide
for the creating and establishment of a national
park or national parks under such terms and con-
ditions as have been or may be provided by any act
of Congress of the United States relating thereto ,
and particularly to do or cause to be done all neces-
sary acts and deeds required by an act of the Con-
gress of the United States entitled , " An Act to pro-
vide for the establishment of the Mammoth Cave
National Park in the State of Kentucky , and for
other purposes , as conditions precedent to the
establishment by the United States of the Mammoth
Cave National Park . To accomplish this end it shall
be the duty of the Commission to acquire land , caves ,
cave rights , leasehold interests , money and other
property for public park purposes , and to convey
same to the United States for the establishment of
such national park or parks , it being necessary , in
order to obtain the advantages and benefits to the

addition to the powers and duties heretofore confer-
red or imposed upon it by law , shall have full power
and authority to acquire , by the exercise of the

recreational utility, or historic interest or remark-
able natural phenomena , or any other unusual
features which , in the judgment of said Commission ,
expressed by resolution , or in the judgment of the
General Assembly , expressed either by Act or by
Joint Resolution , should be acquired or preserved
and maintained for the purposes of the law creating
and governing the said Kentucky State Park Com-
mission .
Said Kentucky State Park Commission shall have
power, and when so directed by Act or Resolution
of the General Assembly it shall be its duty , to insti-
tute and prosecute on behalf of the State any con-
demnation proceedings necessary or proper for the
acquisition of any such properties for State park
purposes that cannot be acquired on reasonable

spects substantially in accordance with the laws re-

Board of Commissioners of any city of the second
class shall deem it proper and expedient to pur-
chase property within said city , or within two miles
from the city limits thereof , to be used as a public
park , the said council or Board of Commissioners
may , by ordinance , submit to the qualified voters of
the city at the next regular election the question
whether bonds of the city shall be issued for the
purpose or purposes aforesaid . The bonds so issued
shall be issued as Park Improvement Bonds and the
ordinance shall fix the time the bonds shall run and
if a serial issue , then the amount to mature at each
time , and the rate of interest which shall be permit-

be issued , and provide for a levy of taxes to pay the

issue . And the council or Board of Commissioners
shall at once adopt an ordinance in conformity
therewith and cause the bonds to at once be prop-
erly prepared for sale and delivery ; provided that
no such bonds shall be sold for less than par ; and
provided further that any premium that shall be ob-
tained from such bonds shall constitute a part of

classification.summary: This act amends Section 2061 of the Kentucky Statutes (Carroll's 1915 Edition) concerning the State Board of Health. The Public Health Unit is to be organized according to the state's Public Health Laws.

WHEREAS , it appears that certain moneys , of considerable
amount , were collected by the United States of America by
direct ad valorem taxes levied by said Government upon
property situated in Kentucky , subsequent to the Civil War
of 1861-65 , and more especially in the years 1866 , 1867 and
1868 ; and
WHEREAS , since said taxes were so levied and collected , it has
been held by the Supreme Court of the United States that the
imposition and collection by the United States of such direct
taxes was unauthorized and illegal , but no part of the moneys
so assessed and collected has ever been repaid to the original
taxpayers or their legal representatives , but measures to that
end have been initiated , as evidenced by a joint resolution in-
troduced and now or lately pending in the Congress of the
United States ; and
WHEREAS , under authority conferred upon him by existing
laws of the Commonwealth , the Governor of Kentucky has em-
ployed special counsel to represent the interests of the Com-
monwealth and the taxpayers owning property situated in Ken-
tucky upon which such direct taxes were imposed and col-
lected by the . United States , or the legal representatives of
such taxpayrs ; and
WHEREAS , it is deemed desirable and proper that the action
of the Governor of this Commonwealth in so proceeding as
aforesaid be ratified , enforced and rendered more effectual ,

CHAPTER 154 .
AN ACT fixing the time in which an action shall be commenced
for the purpose of setting aside a tax deed , executed by the
sheriff , or Auditor of Public Accounts to the purchaser of real
property at a tax sale or to recover the title or possession
thereof when the owner of such property has failed to pay any
State or county taxes thereon for a period of five consecutive
years before said tax sale is made .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. When the owner of any real property shall
fail to pay the State and County taxes due thereon
for a period of five consecutive years , and said prop-
erty has been sold for taxes , after the expiration of
said period by the Sheriff or Auditor of Public Ac-
counts and deeds executed to the purchaser at any
such tax sale , no action in law or in equity shall be
commenced by said owner , his heirs or assigns to set
aside any such tax deed or to recover the title or pos-
session of said property after five years , from the
time such deed is executed , delivered and lodged for
record in the County Clerk's office , in the County
where said property is situated and this limitation
shall apply to all such tax deeds now on record , pro-
vided , however , that an infant may bring any such
action within one year after arriving at the age of
twenty -one years . This limitation shall not apply to
a person of unsound mind or out of the United
States , while in the service of or employed of (by)
the United States at the time the cause of action
accrued but the disability of one of several claimants
shall save only his own right and not that of another .
$ 2. All laws or parts of laws in conflict herewith
are to the extent of such conflict hereby repealed .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That notices of liens for taxes payable to the
United States of America , and certificates discharg-
ing such liens , shall be filed by the collector of inter-
nal revenue , in duplicate , in the office of the county
clerk of the county or counties in the State within
which the property subject to such lien is situated .
§ 2. When a notice of such tax lien is filed , the
county clerk shall forthwith enter the same in an
alphabetical Federal Tax Lien Index , showing on
one line the name and residence of the taxpayer
named in such notice , the collector's serial number
of such notice , the date and hour of filing , and the
amount of tax and penalties . He shall endorse on
both the original and duplicate copies of the notice
the date and hour of filing and shall mail the dupli-
cate to the collector of internal revenue from whom
received . The county clerk shall file and keep all
original notices so filed , in numerical order , in a file
or files designated " Federal Tax Lien Notices . "
3. When a certificate of discharge of any tax
lien issued by the collector of internal revenue is
filed in the office of the county clerk where the origi-
nal notice of said lien is filed , said county clerk shall
enter the same , with the date of filing , in the said
Federal Tax Lien Index , on the same line where the
notice of the lien so discharged is entered and shall
permanently attach the original certificate of dis-
charge to the original notice of lien . He shall mail

classification.title: CHAPTER 156
classification.summary: This statute outlines the qualifications and restrictions for members of a commission appointed to oversee roads, highways, and bridges in Kentucky. It specifies that members must be citizens and residents of Kentucky, at least thirty years old, and well-informed about state infrastructure needs. It prohibits individuals who are employed by or have financial interests in companies that supply materials for road construction, reconstruction, repair, or maintenance from serving on the commission. It also prevents commissioners, engineers, superintendents, or foremen from holding other public offices or being members of political committees while serving on the commission or in its employ.
classification.reasoning: The statute focuses on the qualifications and ethical conduct of individuals appointed to a commission responsible for infrastructure projects. It details requirements such as residency, age, knowledge of state needs, and prohibitions against conflicts of interest with material suppliers. It also restricts commission members from holding other public offices or engaging in political committee activities. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 162.
AN ACT relating to the building and maintaining of roads by
the State Highway Commission to permanent landing fields
operated by municipalities , counties , or state .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the State Highway Commission is
hereby authorized and empowered to construct and
maintain roads leading to permanent landing fields
operated by municipalities , counties or state to con-
nect with state roads constructed and maintained
by said Highway Commission now built and main-
tained or which may hereafter be built .
§ 2. Said roads leading to said air ports or land-
ing fields shall not exceed one mile in length from
main roads maintained by said State Highway Com-
mission .
§ 3. Said road to be of type and construction to
be determined by said State Highway Commission ,
the width of surfacing to be not less than eighteen
(18) feet.
§ 4. Said Highway Commission is to build said
road when and upon the certification of the Air
Board of Kentucky .
§ 5. The State Highway Commission is to be
governed in constructing these roads by the statutes
controlling other State primary road projects .
§ 6. The state or county shall have the same
right to obtain land for right of way purposes as in
other primary roads .
Neither approved nor disapproved .

It also outlines miscellaneous expenses to be covered, including costs of suits, attorneys' fees, conveyance of lunatics to asylums, commissions on fines, compensation for tax commissioners, costs of criminal prosecutions, clerk services, public books, cemetery care, allowances for the "idiots" (likely referring to individuals with intellectual disabilities), interest on warrants, jury commissioner per diem, refunds of Jefferson County fees, and law library expenses.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details appropriations for a progress commission and a list of miscellaneous expenses. None of the listed expenses or appropriations mention race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. The term 'idiots' is used, which historically could have been applied to individuals with intellectual disabilities, but there is no indication in this text that it was racially discriminatory. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

propriations and shall incur no indebtedness exceed-

missions and institutions set out in this Act shall

priated by this Act and no overdrafts of past fiscal
years, except as provided for in this Act , shall be
carried over into this biennial period .

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reenact Section 2744 of the Kentucky Statutes, Relating to Water Districts
classification.summary: This statute outlines a system for classifying land within water districts based on the benefits received from water services. It establishes five classes (A through E) with corresponding assessment ratios (5:4:3:2:1) and allows for adjustments to these classes if strict adherence would cause substantial injustice to landowners. The report of the water district commissioners must include estimated costs, a description of lands and owners, and estimated benefits to each class of property.

It establishes a tiered system based on the benefit received from water services and outlines the proportional assessment rates for each tier. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header '596 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1930.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. Defining Plumber , Master Plumber and
Journeyman Plumber .-Plumbing is the art of in-
stalling in buildings the pipes for distributing the
water supply , the fixtures for using water , and
drainage pipes for removing waste water and sewer-
age , together with fittings and appurtenances of
various kinds all within or adjacent to the building
and including also the " service pipe " which forms
the connection between the shut off valve and curb
line and the building , and the " house sewer " which
conveys the waste water and sewage from the build-
ing to the street sewer.or other point of disposal , in-
cluding private sewage disposal contraptions or
other methods and also includes storm or rain water
pipings , if same is discharged through or connected
with house sewer or house drain . In order to safe-
guard life , health and property , any person , firm or
corporation engaged in or working at the business
of plumbing , either as a master plumber or a jour-
neyman plumber , in counties containing cities of the
first and second class of this Commonwealth , shall
hereafter be required to submit evidence that he or
she is qualified and shall be required to register , as
hereinafter provided .
A master plumber is hereby defined to be a person
who assumes responsible charge and direction of
other persons in the installation of plumbing and
drainage and is skilled in the planning , superintend-
ing and the practical installation of plumbing and
drainage .
A journeyman plumber is hereby defined to be a
person who performs the work of installing plumb-
ing and drainage under the direction of a master
plumber.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1930, p. 678

classification.summary: This act defines plumbing, master plumbers, and journeyman plumbers. It requires individuals engaged in plumbing in cities of the first and second class to submit evidence of qualification and register. The purpose is to safeguard life, health, and property.

It focuses on professional qualifications and public safety. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for the plumbing and drainage industry, requiring registration for individuals in responsible charge and prohibiting the employment of unregistered individuals for gain. It exempts maintenance men and employees of water companies from these provisions. The State Board of Health is empowered to enforce the act, issue and revoke certificates, and create necessary rules and regulations.

§ 4. Materials-Kind That May Be Used .- The
use of all unsafe and defective material in the work
of plumbing and drainage is prohibited . Only the
best known methods of installing of materials and
fixtures, including supply pipes , waste , ventilation ,

be unlawful to use in the work aforesaid extra light
lead pipe, bends and traps , and all combination lead
and iron or combination lead and brass ferrules ,
bends or traps ; but there shall be employed only the
best known methods of joining lead or iron pipes by
the use of heavy cast brass ferrules properly wiped
with solder to lead .

classification.summary: This act amends a previous law regulating the practice of architecture in Kentucky. It outlines the qualifications and requirements for individuals to be issued a certificate as a Registered Architect without examination. These qualifications include being a citizen, at least 21 years of age, having practiced architecture for at least one year prior to the act's passage, having at least ten years of satisfactory experience, or holding a diploma from a recognized architecture school, or being registered in another state with reciprocal privileges.

§ 5. The following restrictions are placed on the
operations of employment agencies :
(a ) No employment agency shall send or direct
an applicant for employment to any fictitious job or
position and no employment agency shall make any
false representation concerning any matter within
the scope of the business of the employment agency
and the non -existence of such job or position or the
falsity of any such representation shall constitute
prima facie evidence of the violation of this section .
(b) No employment agency shall send an appli-
cant for employment to any place where a strike or
any labor trouble exists or is pending without first
notifying the applicant of such condition in writing
and if sent uninformed to such a locality it shall
constitute prima facie evidence of the violation of
this section .
( c ) No employment agency shall circulate any
false or misleading information by advertisement in
newspapers or periodicals , signs , letters , posters ,
cards , or in any other way ; or make any false state-
ment or misrepresentation to any person seeking
employment or to any employer seeking an em-
ployee .
(d ) No employment agency shall enter into any
agreement whatsoever with any employer or his
agent or employee to secure the discharge of an
employee ; nor shall an employer or any one in his
employ or representing it , give or receive any gra-
tuity , divide , or offer to divide , or share directly or
indirectly , any fee , charge or compensation received
from an applicant for employment .

entry or statement in any record or in any receipt

classification.title: An Act relating to the practice of embalming.
classification.summary: This act outlines the requirements for obtaining a license to practice embalming in Kentucky. Applicants must be at least 21 years old, possess a high school education (evidenced by a diploma or credits), and have completed a 26-week course in embalming, disinfection, and sanitation from a recognized school. They must also submit a written application with a $25 fee, provide detailed information about their qualifications, and furnish two certificates of good moral character signed by a licensed embalmer and undertaker. The State Board of Embalming will then schedule an examination.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the professional licensing requirements for embalmers in Kentucky. It specifies age, educational, and training prerequisites. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The statute also prohibits the purchase or receipt of metallic articles through sale, barter, or exchange.

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
classification.summary: This section of the Acts of the General Assembly from 1930 outlines the powers and responsibilities of a board of visitors for an institution. It details their authority to set rules for pupils, regulate tuition fees and admission terms for out-of-state pupils, and establish by-laws. The board is required to meet monthly and can elect a president pro tempore and a secretary pro tempore if needed. They must also receive an annual report from the superintendent detailing the institution's condition, employee information, pupil demographics, financial receipts, and expenditures. The board also has the power to elect a treasurer.

CHAPTER 182 .
AN ACT to extend and prescribe the rights of any railroad cor-
poration . company , board of trustees , or other body , that have
built and now own a railroad in this state , under a franchise
or grant limited as to time .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
§ 1. That any railroad corporation , company ,
Board of Trustees , or other body , heretofore grant-
ed the right or power to build and maintain a rail-
road in this State , for a limited number of years ,
and which now owns a railroad in this State , built
and maintained pursuant to such grant or power ,
shall henceforth have all the same rights and privi-
leges as are conferred by present and future general
statutes of this State upon railroad corporations
and may exercise , and continue to exercise , the same
without regard to and despite any limitations of
years contained in any such original franchise or
grant .
§ 2. That all laws or parts of laws in conflict
herewith are hereby repealed .
Approved March 20 , 1930 .

CHAPTER 188 .
AN ACT to provide for the change in name of a child adopted
pursuant to Sections 2071 and 2072 , of the Kentucky Statutes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky :
When an infant shall be or shall have been adopted
and given parental control as provided in Sections
two thousand and seventy -one ( 2071 ) and two thous-
and and seventy -two ( 2072 ) of the Kentucky Stat-
utes ( Carroll's 1922 Edition ) , on petition of the per-
son so adopting such infant , filed in the circuit court
of the county of his residence , the surname of such
child may , in the discretion of the Judge , be changed
to that of the person adopting him . The judgment ,
if the change be ordered , shall show the original
name , age and place of birth of the child and the
name to which the change is made , and the names of
the child's father and mother , if known , and of the
person whose name he is assuming .
Approved March 25 , 1930 .
